coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nanocellulose-based hydrogel incorporating titanate nanofibers for sorption and detection of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Junwen
Yuan, Hanmeng
Ren, Tingting
Liu, Zhihuan
Qiao, Jianzheng
Ma, Qiang
Guo, Xin
Ma, Guoxin
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: Chromium pollution is a major environmental concern; thus, effective and multifunctional adsorbents for removing the Cr(VI) ion are urgently needed. A fluorescent nanocellulose-based hydrogel (FNH) incorporating titanate nanofibers (TNs) was developed for the sorption and detection of Cr(VI) ion. The chemical and physical structures of the hydrogels, as well as their sorption and detection properties, were studied. The predicted maximum adsorption capacity and the lowest detection limit of FNH were 648.4 mg/g and 0.039 μg/L, respectively. Furthermore, the sorption and detection mechanisms of FNH were discussed in detail. These results showed that the excellent sorption and detection might be mainly attributed to the three-dimensional (3D) porous structure constructed by TNs and cellulose nanocrystals modified with carbon dots, which improved the sorption ability and provided the rapid visual response to Cr(VI). Furthermore, cost analysis showed that FNH was cheaper than activated carbon in removing the Cr(VI) ion. This work established a facile technique in developing low-cost and multifunctional adsorbents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanocrystals
Hydrogel
Titanate nanofibers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CNs modified with CDs (CNs–CDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis scheme for the FNH.

(a) SEM image. (b) Visible light image. (c) XRD analysis. (d) FTIR analysis. (e) UV–Vis spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. (f) The excitation-dependent characteristics. (g) Fluorescent behavior for the prepared FNHs.

(a) Effect of pH on Cr(VI) sorption: T = 25 °C, V = 100 mL, C0 = 40 mg/L, t = 48 h and m = 0.10 g/L. (b) Effect of temperature on Cr(VI) sorption: pH = 2, V = 100 mL, C0 = 40 mg/L, t = 48 h and m = 0.10 g/L. (c) Effect of dosage on Cr(VI) sorption: pH = 2, T = 25 °C, V = 100 mL, C0 = 40 mg/L and t = 48 h.
(a) Sorption isotherm of Cr(VI) on FNH-5: pH = 2, T = 25 °C, V = 100 mL, t = 48 h, and m = 0.10 g/L. (b-e) Fitting curves from Langmuir and Freundlich, Temkin and D–R models.
(a) Time-dependent Cr(VI) sorption on FNH-5: pH = 2, T = 25 °C, V = 100 mL, C0 = 40 mg/L and m = 0.10 g/L. (b-c) Fitting curves from Pseudo-first-order and Pseudo-second-order models.
Comparison of fluorescence (a) and QY (b) of FNH-5 before and after sorption of 10 metal ions.
QY (a) and fluorescent emission spectra (b) of FNH-5 upon addition of various concentrations of Cr(VI).
(a) FTIR spectra. (b) XPS survey. (c-e) Cr2p, N1s, O1s and Ti2p high-resolution spectra.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) solution and fluorescence excitation spectra of FNH-5. (b) Fluorescence decay spectra of FNH-5.
Adsorption capacities in comparison with previous work.
Comparison of sensing performance for Cr(VI).

row.entry: Carbon dots prepared from Tulsi leaves

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of Cr(VI) in tap water and pool water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nanocellulose-based hydrogel incorporating titanate nanofibers for sorption and detection of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Computer and Control Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai, Shandong 264005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Computer and Control Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering
Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha, 410004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chromium pollution is a major environmental concern; thus, effective and multifunctional adsorbents for removing the Cr(VI) ion are urgently needed. A fluorescent nanocellulose-based hydrogel (FNH) incorporating titanate nanofibers (TNs) was developed for the sorption and detection of Cr(VI) ion. The chemical and physical structures of the hydrogels, as well as their sorption and detection properties, were studied. The predicted maximum adsorption capacity and the lowest detection limit of FNH were 648.4 mg/g and 0.039 μg/L, respectively. Furthermore, the sorption and detection mechanisms of FNH were discussed in detail. These results showed that the excellent sorption and detection might be mainly attributed to the three-dimensional (3D) porous structure constructed by TNs and cellulose nanocrystals modified with carbon dots, which improved the sorption ability and provided the rapid visual response to Cr(VI). Furthermore, cost analysis showed that FNH was cheaper than activated carbon in removing the Cr(VI) ion. This work established a facile technique in developing low-cost and multifunctional adsorbents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are critical pollutants in wastewater. One of the heavy metals, Cr(VI) ion, can cause serious public health problems when it is released into water because of the generation of antimicrobial-resistant bacteria, which has adverse effects on the aquatic environment [1]. Hence, Cr(VI) ions have become a well-known environmental concern. Various water treatment technologies have been established for removing Cr(VI) ions, such as adsorption [2,3], photocatalysis [4] and membrane filtration [5]. Adsorption is a useful technique in which the adsorbents are critical. Designing and synthesising highly effective adsorbents for removing the Cr(VI) ions are essential demands based on the environment and sustainable development strategy [6].

Recently, the use of nanocellulose-based hydrogel as an adsorbent has attracted great attention. Pei et al. fabricated a cellulose-based composite adsorbent via facile cross-linking of polyethyleneimine/polyacrylamide (PEI/PAM) and cellulose using epichlorohydrin as the cross-linker. They subsequently showed that the Langmuir model was preferred to illustrate the hydrogel isotherm [7]. Wang et al. synthesised a cellulose hydrogel coating nanoscale Fe0; the hydrogel's performance in removing Cr(VI) ion was evaluated through a comparative experiment [8]. Kim et al. prepared a cationic surface-modified regenerated nanocellulose hydrogel to remove Cr(VI) ion and found an excellent removal capacity of 578 mg/g [9]. Subsequently, the incorporating of nanomaterials has been used to improve the hydrogel's sorption performance. Based on this, the introduced titanate nanofibers (TNs) served as the hydrogel's skeleton while increasing sorption sites [10,11]. Therefore, the hydrogel incorporating TNs has a significant potential to develop effective adsorbents [12].

Carbon dots (CDs) are of keen interest to many researchers owing to their good water solubility, low toxicity, and ideal fluorescence properties [13–15]. In many literature, CDs are suitable for detecting heavy metals, like Cr(VI) ion [16]. Zhang et al. used a signal shut-off probe of Si, N-codoped CDs to detect Cr(VI) ion by fluorescence quenching, and it showed that CDs had a high sensitivity for detecting Cr(VI) with a linear range of 0–200 μm and the detection limit of 0.995 μm [17]. Wang et al. developed an ionic sensor based on nitrogen-doped CDs for sensitive detection of Cr(VI) ions by fluorescence spectrometry [18]. Laddha et al. synthesised blue light-emitting fluorescent N–S–P co-doped carbon dots (PGP-CDs) as a nanoprobe for sequential detection of Cr(VI) ion by a simple, yet rapid procedure using microwave charring [19]. However, as a fluorescent sensor, CDs are weakly stable and easily aggregate [20]. Immobilizing CDs in a suitable matrix is a promising way to overcome these problems.

A novel FNH incorporating TNs was fabricated. The morphology, chemical composition and optical features of the FNH were investigated. The sorption and detection performances of the FNH toward Cr(VI) ion removal were observed. Then, the Cr(VI) sorption and detection mechanisms for FNH were analysed. Moreover, the economic feasibility of FNH over activated carbon was examined.

2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO, 98 %), 1,2-Ethanediamine (EDA, 98 %), N′,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 99 %), acrylic acid (AA, AR grade) and Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5 %) were bought from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd.

Bleached wood pulp (20 g) with α-cellulose content of 92.3 % was dispersed in deionised water (360.0 g). Then, TEMPO (0.16 g), NaBr (0.18 g) and NaClO (12.0 g) were mixed thoroughly. The pH was changed to 10.5. Then, the oxidation process was conducted for 7 h. The product was neutralised before being passed through an M110P microfluidiser five times, yielding CNs with a concentration of 0.5 wt%. The CNs' weight-average molecular weight, viscosity and carboxyl content were 19,306, 3.1 dL/g and 0.98 mmol/g, respectively.

CDs were fabricated via typical method [21]. EDA (268 uL) and CA solution (5 mL, 0.042 g/mL) were mixed well. Then, the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave, heated at 300 °C for 5 h and cooled to room temperature. Then, CDs crude product was centrifugated for 10 min at 12000 rpm to remove supernatant liquid. Finally, an appropriate amount of deionised water was added and the concentration of CDs solution was 0.1 wt%. A high-resolution TEM image further confirmed the generation of CDs (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CNs modified with CDs (CNs–CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CNs (20 g, 0.5 wt%) were mixed well with MES buffer of pH 6.5 (5 mL, 0.2 M). Then, N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 60 mg) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 230 mg) were added with stirring for 30 min. The prepared CDs solution (15 mg) was then combined, stirred and mixed thoroughly for 12 h at 25 °C. The products were dialysed against deionised water for 4 d. Lastly, an appropriate amount of deionised water was added, and the concentration of CNs–CDs suspension was adjusted to 0.5 wt%.

TNs were fabricated via typical method [11]. Titanyl sulfate solution (30 g, 12 wt%) and NaOH solution (67 mL, 15 M) were mixed. Then, the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 48 h and cooled to room temperature. The synthesised TNs were neutralised with deionised water. Finally, TNs powder was obtained using freeze-drying.

Different qualities of CNs–CDs (1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5 and 4.0 g) were put into a conical flask, respectively. Then, AA (2.5 g), NaOH (10 mL, 5 mol/L), TNs (0.24 g), N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 0.03 g) and potassium persulfate (0.01 g) were placed in nitrogenous atmosphere. The reaction lasted 12 h at 40 °C to get the hydrogel product. Then, the hydrogel product was washed with deionised water to remove unreacted reagents. A series of hydrogel products were labelled FNH-1, FNH-2, FNH-3, FNH-4, FNH-5, FNH-6 and FNH-7. The maximum adsorption capacities of these FNHs were obtained; the FNH-5 had the highest value (Fig.S2). Consequently, FNH-5 was used for subsequent experiments. Furthermore, original hydrogel (OH) was synthesised using the same method without the CNs–CDs and TNs.

Batch experiments were used to measure Cr(VI) ion adsorption on FNH-5 and OH. FNH-5 and OH samples were submerged separately in 250 mL conical flasks containing Cr(VI) ion solutions at various concentrations. The conical flasks were then fixed on a thermostatic shaker at a preset temperature and residence time. After that, 10 mL of the solution was collected and filtered using syringe filters, and its concentration was determined by atomic absorption spectrophotometer (TAS-990, Beijing's General Instrument, China). All the adsorption data were the average of triplicate determinations.

The synthesis scheme for the FNH is shown in Fig. 1 . CNs, CDs and TNs were three kinds of main raw materials. First, a condensation reaction was performed to form CNs–CDs based on the raw material CNs and CDs. Then, CNs–CDs, AA, TNs and MBA cross-linking agents were stirred and blended thoroughly. Then, a potassium persulfate initiator was added. Finally, free-radical polymerisation reaction was conducted to generate FNH in which the 3D porous structure was constructed and more sorption sites were provided.

Fig. 2a–b shows the 3D porous structure of FNH. After radical polymerisation, the 3D porous structure could be constructed, and CDs were wrapped [22]. Furthermore, the 3D porous structure of FNH was confirmed by the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm (Fig. S3).

Fig. 2c shows the XRD patterns of TNs, CNs, CNs–CDs and FNH. TNs displayed eight characteristic diffraction peaks from sodium titanate [23]. Meanwhile, CNs showed three peaks (22.6°, 16.2° and 14.9°) relating to the crystal structure of cellulose [24–26]. The abovementioned CNs–CDs peaks did not appear in FNH because of polymerisation. Meanwhile, other peaks from TNs could be found probably due to the TNs only being wrapped to the 3D porous structure of FNH [27].

Fig. 2d shows the FTIR spectra of TNs, CNs, CNs–CDs and FNH. The CNs had one strong absorption peak which was attributed to O
H stretching vibration at 3365 cm−1 [28]. Meanwhile, CNs–CDs and FNH had one strong absorption peak attributed to O
H and N
H stretching vibrations at 3350 cm−1 [29]. Additionally, a significant characteristic band (1054 cm−1) associated with the CO band of cellulose was observed in CNs and CNs–CDs [30]. Characteristic peak at 909 cm−1 was due to the TiO stretching vibration for TNs [31]. The characteristic peak at 1650 cm−1 was due to the –CO–NH– stretching vibration for CNs–CDs, indicating that CDs were connected to CNs [32]. The peak at 1054 cm−1 did not appear in FNH, suggesting that CNs–CDs occurred during polymerisation [33]. Meanwhile, the peak at 909 cm−1 was found in FNH, confirming that TNs being wrapped.

The optical properties were investigated. The UV–vis spectra of the FNH-5 had two obvious peaks at 282 nm and 354 nm which were attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions of CDs (Fig. 2e) [34]. Maximum photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission peaks of FNH were at 394 and 477 nm, respectively. When the excitation wavelength changed from 334 to 454 nm, there was an obvious red-shift in the wavelength range of 449–541 nm (Fig. 2f). The result was consistent with the previous report about CDs [35].

A series of the prepared FNHs showed blue fluorescence with different intensities (Fig. 2g). The fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased, as CNs–CDs increased. Furthermore, FNH-5 exhibited the strongest fluorescence and had a 13.5 % quantum yield (QY). The method of calculating the QY is provided in Supplementary Material.

The pH is an important factor that directly affects Cr(VI) adsorption. Fig. 3a shows the adsorption capacity (Q e) of FNH-5 as a function of pH. Q e increased as the pH increased from 1 to 2 and decreased as the pH increased from 2 to 6. This change was attributed to the ionic state of the Cr(VI). In the acidic pH range of 1–6, the functional group of FNH-5 was protonated (Fig. S4). At the pH of 2, Cr(VI) had a negative charge, and there was a strong electrostatic attraction between FNH-5 and Cr(VI). Therefore, the Q e of FNH-5 was high. However, at higher initial pH (> 2), the Q e of FNH-5 decreased. The decrease in Q e may be due to a reduction in the degree of protonation of amine groups, resulting in a reduction in the number of positive charges on the FNH-5. Therefore, subsequent experiments were performed at a pH of 2.

The effect of FNH-5 dosage was examined. Fig. 3b shows the removal efficiency as a function of FNH-5 dosage. The removal efficiency increased rapidly with the increase in FNH-5 dosage from 0.02 to 0.10 g/L. This change was attributed to the rise in adsorption sites [36]. The removal efficiency slowly increased once the FNH-5 dosage was higher than 0.10 g/L. To save the adsorbent, the FNH-5 dosage of 0.10 g/L was selected.

Temperature also has a distinct effect on Cr(VI) sorption. Here, the temperature was varied to analyse its effect (Fig. 3c). Q e increased with temperature in the range of 15 °C–45 °C, confirming that Cr(VI) sorption was endothermic. High temperatures can enhance the diffusion velocity of Cr(VI), favouring Cr(VI) sorption onto FNH. For convenience, all subsequent experiments were conducted at 25 °C. The thermodynamic parameters including the Gibbs free energy (ΔG), enthalpy (ΔH) and entropy (ΔS), can be calculated from the linear fitting of ln(Q e/C e) versus 1/T and Van't Hoff equations (Fig. 3c inset). The values of ΔH and ΔS were 3.013 kJ/mol and 11.75 J/(mol K), respectively. ΔH explained the endothermic nature of the adsorption process, and the positive value of ΔS reflected a good affinity of Cr(VI) toward the FNH-5. Furthermore, the ΔG value ranging from −723.5 to −371 J/mol confirmed the spontaneity and physisorption of the sorption process for Cr(VI) ion on the FNH-5.

The effect of the initial Cr(VI) concentration was examined. Fig. 4a shows Q e of FNH-5 and OH as a function of Cr(VI) concentration. At the same concentration, Q e of FNH-5 was greater than that of OH, indicating that the reconstructed porous structure and increased sorption sites of FNH-5 favoured the sorption of Cr(VI) [37]. The isotherms of FNH-5 were fitted using the Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin and Dubinin–Radushkevich (D–R) models. The results are listed in Fig. 4b–e and Table S1, respectively. The higher value of R2 for the Langmuir model suggested that monolayer sorption occurred. Furthermore, the Langmuir model could estimate the maximum metal uptake upon complete saturation of the sorbent (i.e., theoretical maximum adsorption capacity, Q max). The Q max of FNH-5 was determined to be 648.4 mg/g, corresponding to the experimental maximum adsorption capacity of 638.2 mg/g. The maximum adsorption capacity of FNH-5 was higher than that of other adsorbents reported in the literatures (Table 1 ).

Fig. 5a shows the Q t of FNH-5 and OH as a function of contact time. The Q t of FNH-5 was greater than that of OH, indicating that Cr(VI) sorption was favoured by CDs and TNs. Moreover, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were used to fit the kinetic data of FNH-5, and the results are shown in Fig. 5b–c and Table S2. The R2 for the pseudo-first-order model was lower than that of the pseudo-second-order model, indicating that Cr(VI) sorption on FNH-5 followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, and the rate-limiting step may include the chemical sorption [45].

The ability of adsorbents to regenerate is critical when considering their practical applications. Thus, we investigated Cr(VI) desorption on FNH-5 using NaOH solution (0.1 M). FNH-5 was used consecutively (Fig. S5), and Q m decreased only by 17 % after the four cycles. This result confirmed the good regeneration capability of FNH-5. Thus, FNH-5 can economically and effectively treat Cr(VI).

The performance of FNH-5 in detecting ten metal ions was examined to determine its selectivity. As shown in Fig. 6a and b, Cd2+, Co2+, Na+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Cr(VI), Al3+, Pb2+ and Cu2+ at a concentration of 2000 mg/L changed the fluorescence and QY of FNH-5. These ions had little impact, except for Cr(VI). These results indicated that FNH-5 was unsusceptible to interference and effective for high-selectivity Cr(VI) detection.

Furthermore, the sensitivity of FNH-5 to Cr(VI) was investigated. The fluorescence and QY of FNH-5 decreased as Cr(VI) concentration increased (Fig. 7a). A linear expression of QY with respect to Cr(VI) concentration was obtained over a wide region of 15–100 mg/L (R2 = 0.99441). Then, the fluorescence spectra of FNH-5 at lower Cr(VI) concentrations were analysed. Fig. 7b shows the emission spectra of FNH-5. The emission intensity decreased as Cr(VI) concentration increased. Good linearity (R2 = 0.99254) was observed in the concentration range of 0–1.0 μg/L (Fig. 7b, inset). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.039 μg/L (S/N = 3), below the US EPA maximum permissible Cr(VI) level for drinking water. Table 2 compares FNH-5 with other fluorescence techniques for Cr(VI) detection based on LOD and the linear range. The sensitivity of FNH-5 to Cr(VI) was comparable to that of other techniques.

Tap and pool water in our university were analysed to evaluate the feasibility of sensing Cr(VI) in real samples using FNH-5. First, several standard solutions with known Cr(VI) concentrations were added to the two water samples, and FNH-5 was used to measure the Cr(VI) concentration. As shown in Table 3 , 99.27 % and 103.87 % recovery and <4 % relative standard deviations were obtained. The results confirmed that the prepared FNH-5 was helpful for practical detection.

The FTIR spectra of FNH-5 (Fig. 8a) show a characteristic peak attributed to O
H and N
H stretching vibrations at 3350 cm−1. Meanwhile, the FTIR peak at 1560 cm−1 indicated the presence of the carbonyl group (CO, stretching vibration) [36]. It was found that these two peaks were weakened. The absorption peak at 902 cm−1 was considered from Cr species [52]. These changes confirmed the role of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups in Cr(VI) sorption.

XPS was also used to analyse the sorption mechanism of FNH-5. The elemental composition of FNH-5 was determined from the XPS survey scan (Fig. 8b), which showed the presence of C, N, Ti, O and Cr. XPS verified the sorption of Cr(VI) on FNH-5. The Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 spectra confirmed that Cr(VI) was adsorbed on FNH-5.

The Cr2p high-resolution spectra show two strong peaks at 580 and 589 eV belonging to Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 8c). The Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 peaks were fitted using four component peaks. The component peaks at 576.5 and 586.5 eV are attributed to Cr(III), and the other peaks at 579.7 and 588.9 eV are attributed to Cr(VI). The component peaks related to Cr(III) ions increased, which was attributed to the reduction of Cr(VI), indicating that some hydroxyl groups of FNH-5 were oxidised to carbonyl groups.

The N1s spectra of FNH-5 (Fig. 8d) show only one peak at 399.8 eV, ascribed to the NH group, after Cr(VI) sorption, whereas the N1s spectra were fitted using one peak. This result indicated that the amino group participated in Cr(VI) sorption through ionic bonding [53].

Fig. 8e shows the deconvolution of the O1s regions of the XPS spectra. These regions were fitted by two peaks at 532.3 and 531.1 eV, corresponding to oxygen atoms in the forms of carbonyl and hydroxyl groups, respectively [54]. These peaks slightly shifted to higher binding energies from their original positions, confirming a strong binding ability between the carboxyl group and Cr(III).

The Ti2p spectrum of FNH-5 (Fig. 8f) shows only two peaks at 464.1 and 458.2 eV, after Cr(VI) sorption, whereas the Ti2p spectrum was also fitted using two peaks which were moved to lower binding energy. This result indicated that the TNs participated in Cr(VI) sorption through ion exchange.

We further explored the mechanism of Cr(VI) detection. As shown in Fig. 9a, the ultraviolet absorption band of Cr(VI) overlaps with the excitation band of FNH-5, indicating that the fluorescence of FNH-5 may be quenched by Cr(VI) through the inner filter effect [55]. The fluorescence decay spectra of FNH-5 with and without Cr(VI) adsorption were obtained (Fig. 9b–d). The average fluorescence lifetime of FNH-5 changed slightly with the addition of Cr(VI), showing that the fluorescence quenching of FNH-5 followed a static quenching.

The sorption and detection mechanisms of FNH were summarised below based on the above analysis (Fig. 10 ). First, Cr(VI) ions accumulated on the FNH surface due to electrostatic attraction. Then, the Cr(VI) ions spread to the interior of FNH through the constructed 3D porous structure. Meanwhile, a considerable amount of Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III). Then, the generated Cr(III) could be attached to the FNH. Due to the constructed 3D porous structure and increased sorption sites, FNH showed superior Cr(VI) sorption capability. Furthermore, Cr(VI) could contact CDs through electrostatic attraction, resulting in the FNH fluorescence quenching. Therefore, FNH exhibits excellent sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection.

Cost is critical in selecting adsorbents for treating Cr(VI); hence, the cost of FNH-5 was estimated. The preparation cost for FNH-5 includes the cost of raw material, chemical modification agents, and energy [56]. The total and subentry costs are listed in Table S3. The total cost of FNH-5 was CNF 78.66/kg, whereas activated carbon cost CNF 253.0/kg. Based on the adsorption capacity obtained from batch adsorption experiments, the costs of FNH-5 and activated carbon for removing 1 g of Cr(VI) are listed in Table S4. On CNF 0.12 worth of FNH-5 was required to remove 1 g of Cr(VI), which was much less than that of activated carbon (CNF 2.11). Therefore, FNH-5 was a better low-cost adsorbent for Cr(VI) removal.

FNH was successfully synthesised with a 3D porous structure and increased sorption sites for Cr(VI) adsorption and detection. FNH was characterised using various techniques that confirmed its reconstructed porous structure and newly added sorption sites. The equilibrium data fitted well with the Langmuir model, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 648.4 mg/g. According to kinetic analysis, Cr(VI) adsorption followed the pseudo-second-order model. After four adsorption–desorption cycles, the regenerated FNH had an adsorption capacity of 83 %. Further, FNH was used to detect Cr(VI) ions. Moreover, FNH is more cost-effective than activated carbon according to cost analysis. Thus, FNH is a promising low-cost adsorbent for Cr(VI).

Junwen Peng: Investigation. Hanmeng Yuan: Software. Tingting Ren: Software. Zhihuan Liu: Investigation. Jianzheng Qiao: Resources. Qiang Ma: Resources. Xin Guo: Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft, Validation, Supervision. Guoxin Ma: Investigation. Yiqiang Wu: Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financed by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 34890771), Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project (Grant No. 2020RC3043) and Training Plan of Young Backbone Teachers in Colleges and Universities of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 71).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2022.06.148.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CTAB-modified carboxymethyl cellulose/bagasse cryogels for the efficient removal of bisphenol a, methylene blue and Cr(VI) ions: batch and column adsorption studies
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [1] I.P. Meneses, S.D. Novaes, R.S. Dezotti, P.V. Oliveira, D.F. Siqueira Petri, CTAB-modified carboxymethyl cellulose/bagasse cryogels for the efficient removal of bisphenol a, methylene blue and Cr(VI) ions: Batch and column adsorption studies, J. Hazard. Mater. 421 (2022) 126804. doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.126804.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cu-fe embedded cross-linked 3D hydrogel for enhanced reductive removal of Cr(VI): characterization, performance, and mechanisms
Chemosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [2] Y. Wang, N. Lin, Y. Gong, R. Wang, X. Zhang, Cu-Fe embedded cross-linked 3D hydrogel for enhanced reductive removal of Cr(VI): Characterization, performance, and mechanisms, Chemosphere. 280 (2021) 130663. doi:10.1016/j.chemosphere.2021.130663.
